modulePlan.goal: Identify and explain the genetic loci and triggers necessary for a high-scoring etiology section in a 10-mark answer.
concepts: Genetic polymorphisms in IL23R and TNFAIP3
Lifestyle triggers: obesity, smoking, and alcohol

topic.compressedInterview:
Purpose: Post-graduate dermatology exam preparation (specifically structured for a 10-mark theory answer)
Prior knowledge: Dermatology PG student — strong medical foundation, requires high-level immunodermatology details
Desired depth: Advanced — focusing on molecular pathways (IL-23/Th17 axis), genetic susceptibility (PSORS1-9), and feed-forward mechanisms
Time available: 4 months (long-term mastery and retention)
Focus areas: Genetic loci and HLA associations, Trigger-pathophysiology correlation, Molecular feed-forward cycle, Exam-style flowcharts for scoring
Source type: PDF (iavcl-psoriasis.pdf)
topic.rawTranscript:
User: I want to learn about: pathophysiology of psoriasis including genetic factors, risk factors, triggering factors and corelating everything in actual pathophyology and give me in easy, understandable and as a 10 mark answer to the questions (from a PDF: iavcl-psoriasis.pdf)

Assistant: It sounds like you're looking for a very structured, exam-ready breakdown—specifically that "10-mark answer" style. To help me make this as clear as possible for you, are you a medical student, a nursing student, or perhaps studying another health-related field?

User: i am dermatology post graduation student

Assistant: That makes sense—since you're a PG student, we can focus on the higher-level immunopathogenesis and specific molecular pathways like the IL-23/Th17 axis that examiners love. How soon is your exam? Also, for your 10-mark answer, would you like me to emphasize the detailed genetic loci (like PSORS1-9) or focus more on the clinical-pathological correlation of the "feed-forward" cycle?

User: both

Assistant: Understood. Since we're covering both the complex genetic loci (like PSORS1 and the HLA-Cw6 association) and the intricate molecular crosstalk for a PG-level answer, I have one last question to make sure this fits your schedule: How much time do you have to master this—are you in "crunch mode" for an exam this week, or do you have a few days to digest the complexities? (Also, would you like me to include a schematic "flowchart" description that you can easily replicate in your answer script?)

User: i have exam in 4 months but include flowcharts too

Assistant: I have everything I need to build your study plan. Since you're a PG student with a 4-month window, we can focus on building a high-yield, structured "10-mark" framework that integrates those complex genetic details with clear, reproducible flowcharts.
productionSubtopicTitles: The Genetic Landscape: PSORS Loci and Major Polymorphisms
Environmental Catalysts: Physical Trauma and Streptococcal Triggers
Systemic Modifiers: Pharmacological Triggers and Lifestyle Factors
topic.profileSummary: Advanced · PG exam-oriented · 4 months · Molecular depth · 7 concepts

parsed.title: Primary Genetic Susceptibility: The PSORS Loci
parsed.scope: Introduce the foundational genetic basis of psoriasis required for a top-tier PG exam answer. This section will focus extensively on the most critical genetic determinant, the PSORS1 locus, and its strong association with HLA-Cw6 (especially in early-onset disease), followed by a structured overview of the non-PSORS loci (PSORS2-9) to establish the polygenic nature of the disease.

parsed.title: Genetic Foundations: PSORS Loci and HLA Associations
parsed.scope: Introduce the primary genetic architecture of psoriasis, focusing heavily on the foundational PSORS1 locus and its critical HLA-Cw6 association. Provide a structured, high-yield breakdown of the non-PSORS loci (PSORS2-9) to establish the genetic baseline for a 10-mark exam answer.

parsed.title: Genetic Foundations: PSORS Loci and the HLA-Cw6 Association
parsed.scope: This section establishes the primary genetic basis of psoriasis, crucial for the opening of a 10-mark exam answer. It covers the highly penetrant PSORS1 locus, its strong association with the HLA-Cw6 allele, and provides a structured overview of the non-PSORS loci (PSORS2-9).
